meta.setting: cold trail camp near a creek bar and pine brush in Jefferson country
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in track reading and survival evidence

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Rugged road travel and outdoor working conditions.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Black Bear
research_grounding.use: Bear tracks and wild scavenger pressure.

image_prompt.subject: a cold camp with dead ash, empty bedroll dents, scattered tins, a dropped coffee pot, horse tracks, and bear prints near the tree line
image_prompt.scene: early morning blue light after a cold night; no people visible, one saddle blanket hangs on a branch, crows circle far above, no readable labels
image_prompt.composition: square evidence scene with fire ring in foreground, bedroll marks and gear mid-frame, tracks leading toward shadowed brush
image_prompt.camera: low tracker-view angle, close enough to read ash and prints, like an in-engine clue inspection
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make absence visible without making the scene empty.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine trail-evidence scene
render_traits: slightly simplified camp gear, tracks, brush, and birds
modest texture resolution on ash, wool, tin, mud, pine needles, and creek stones
hard ambient occlusion under gear, stones, bedroll dents, and brush
cold dawn light with blue shadows and thin smoke stain
believable 1899 camp objects and trail wear
search-scene composition where tracks and missing people matter
style_modifiers.palette: cold ash gray, blanket brown, tin dull silver, pine green, mud umber, creek blue, and pale dawn gold.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable can labels, paper scraps, brand marks, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The cold ash, bedroll marks, camp gear, horse tracks, bear prints, and brush line are readable.
Every object belongs plausibly to rugged 1899 trail life.
The image supports tracking, missing-person, wildlife, and survival scenes.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
